---
title: "Deviance bias under congenial multiple imputation: a sourced derivation"
subtitle: "Canonical formal derivation for the JAIGP theory paper (setup, assumptions, proof)"
author: "mi-spectral"
date: last-modified
bibliography: ../literature/mi-spectral-better-bibTeX.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    grid:
      margin-width: 350px
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: false
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
  cache: false
---

## Introduction {#sec-intro}

### The problem {#sec-intro-problem}

Multiple imputation (MI) replaces each missing entry with several plausible draws, lets
the analyst apply complete-data tools to each completed data set, and combines the
results [@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. Many of those complete-data tools —
likelihood-ratio statistics, information criteria, profile likelihoods, deviance-based
fit indices — are built, directly or indirectly, on the **imputation-averaged complete-data
log-likelihood**: the Q-function of the EM and congeniality literatures
[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997; @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a].
Write $\bar Q_\infty(\theta)$ for its idealized infinite-imputation limit. One would like
$\bar Q_\infty$ to behave like the complete-data log-likelihood $\ell_{\text{com}}$ that
would have been computed had nothing been missing. It does not. Evaluated at the
observed-data estimator and measured against the complete-data gold standard
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$, the Q-function is systematically biased. This
document derives that bias exactly, to leading order, and names the two mechanisms behind it.

### The result {#sec-intro-result}

Let $\mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ denote the matrix
relative increase in variance due to missingness — Rubin's scalar $r$ in matrix form.
Under congenial, proper MI, taking the complete-data MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ as the
target, the Q-function deviance bias is
$$
\mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big]
= \underbrace{+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{imputation bias}}
\;\;\underbrace{-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{estimation mismatch}}
\;=\; +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}),
$$
to $O(n^{-1})$ (on the deviance scale, double this). The first term is **imputation
bias**: averaging $\ell_{\text{com}}$ over completions drawn from the imputation model
overstates the fit by $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The second is **estimation
mismatch**: MI's estimand is $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, whereas $\bar Q_\infty$ is centered
at the observed-data maximizer $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, and the gap between them costs
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The two do not cancel — they leave a net upward
bias of half the trace. The result holds for any regular likelihood fit on congenially
imputed data; we instantiate it throughout for the multivariate normal.

### Why it matters {#sec-intro-why}

Whatever is built on $\bar Q_\infty$ inherits this bias. Two applications motivate the
paper; both are developed in the manuscript, not here.

- **Likelihood-ratio model comparison.** The MI likelihood-ratio numerator
$\hat d_L = 2[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^*) - \bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi_0^*)]$ — where $\hat\psi^*$
maximizes the averaged log-likelihood, in the sense of
[@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022] — inherits a *differential* bias
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi^*)) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi_0^*))$.
It vanishes under the null but not under local alternatives or non-nested comparison,
distorting power and ranking even when the reference distribution is correctly calibrated
[@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022].
- **Information criteria.** MI-AIC and MI-BIC are built from $-2\bar Q_\infty$, so each
candidate model carries a bias $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ that is *model-specific* —
RIV depends on the model. Left uncorrected, the criteria systematically favor higher-RIV
models; correcting the numerator restores complete-data-equivalent selection, in the same
direction as the complete-data discrepancy correction of
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a] and the missing-data AIC of
[@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008].

### Relation to existing work {#sec-intro-lineage}

The result sits *upstream* of the MI test-calibration literature. Meng and Rubin's
combining rule for likelihood-ratio tests [@mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b], its
refinement by Chan and Meng [@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022], and Chan's general
stacked-MI tests [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] all concern how to *calibrate the
reference distribution* of an MI test statistic, taking the statistic's numerator as
given. We address the *bias of that numerator*. The two are complementary: calibration
makes the reference distribution right; the present correction makes the statistic
referred to it centered. On the model-selection side, Cavanaugh and Shumway's AICcd
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a] and Claeskens and Consentino's missing-data
AIC [@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008] correct the analogous
complete-data discrepancy under EM-based estimation — a parallel line with a different
target estimator but the same RIV machinery. The link is in fact term-by-term: AICcd
targets the complete-data discrepancy at the observed-data (EM) MLE — the same
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ pivot as our Term A — so it carries an imputation-bias penalty in
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ but no analogue of Term B, and our estimation-mismatch
term $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is exactly the additional price of MI
targeting $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ rather than $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$.

### Road map {#sec-intro-roadmap}

@sec-notation fixes notation: the model and missingness, the two likelihoods and their
maximizers, the three Fisher informations and the identity binding them, RIV, the
Q-function, and the bias decomposition $T = A + B$. @sec-ledger collects the regularity,
congeniality, and properness conditions together with the asymptotic lemmas the proof
rests on. @sec-derivation states the theorem, sketches the proof strategy, and gives the
proof: Term B (estimation mismatch) is an elementary Taylor argument, while Term A
(imputation bias) is a second-order expansion turning on a cancellation we isolate as a
lemma. @sec-sources reproduces the cited source passages verbatim, and @sec-verify
verifies the trace identities symbolically and the normal instantiation numerically,
executed inline at render time.

A note on provenance. Because this is an AI-assisted derivation, every external claim is
sourced. The main column carries the mathematics; the right margin carries the provenance
— the source's own equation or theorem number, the reproduced statement, and a line
reference into the local `literature/` copy (with a short verbatim snippet, so the pointer
survives re-pagination). Steps proved here rather than cited are flagged `[derived]`;
those additionally checked symbolically in @sec-verify are flagged `[mc]`. Independently,
the pre-registered Monte Carlo study in `verification/` estimates
$\mathbb{E}[A],\mathbb{E}[B],\mathbb{E}[T]$ across configurations and compares them with the
analytic $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.

---

## Setup and notation {#sec-notation}

This section fixes the objects the theorem is about: the data and model, the two
likelihoods and their maximizers, the three Fisher informations and the identity binding
them, the relative increase in variance, the Q-function, and the bias decomposition
$T = A + B$. The conditions and asymptotic results the proof *rests on* — regularity,
congeniality, properness, and the expansions — are collected separately in @sec-ledger.
Definitions carry a label (D1, D2, …) so that the margin provenance and the later proof
can point back to them.

### Data, model, and missingness {#sec-data}

**D1 (data and model).** Let $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times p}$ collect $N$ complete
observations, the rows drawn independently from a $p$-variate normal,
$Y_i \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu,\Sigma)$ with $\Sigma \succ 0$. The estimand is the
mean–covariance parameter $\theta = (\mu, \operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$, of dimension
$k = p + \tfrac12 p(p+1)$. Missingness, introduced next, will keep us from observing $Y$
in full.

::: {.column-margin}
**Convention.** Complete data $Y=(Y_{\text{obs}},Y_{\text{mis}})$ follows
@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1477</code></a>, where $\ell(\theta\mid Y)$,
$\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})$, $P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$ appear
and @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, <a href="#sec-src-little"><code>:438</code></a>. Meng's congeniality framework writes
$Z_c=\{X,Y_{\text{inc}},I\}$, $Z_o=\{X,Y_{\text{obs}},I,R_{\text{inc}}\}$ with $X$ =
always-observed covariates [@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a], <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:95</code></a>
— a different $X$, which is why we avoid the symbol. The verification code
(`verification/00-setup.R`) labels the realized complete matrix `X`; that is our $Y$.
:::

**D2 (missingness).** A response indicator $R$ (with $1$ marking a missing entry) splits
each row into its observed and missing parts, $Y=(Y_{\text{obs}},Y_{\text{mis}})$.
Throughout we assume the missingness is ignorable — missing at random, with the
missingness parameters distinct from $\theta$ — so the mechanism itself need not be
modeled. The precise conditions are stated as assumptions in @sec-ledger.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinInferenceMissingData1976b, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1976"><code>:39</code></a> and @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, <a href="#sec-src-little"><code>:3263</code></a>: ignorability (MAR + parameter distinctness).
:::

### Likelihoods and estimators {#sec-likelihoods}

Missingness forces a distinction the whole argument turns on: the likelihood we *would*
have maximized with complete data versus the one we *can* maximize from what is observed,
and their two different maximizers.

**D3 (complete-data log-likelihood and MLE).** Had $Y$ been observed in full, we would form
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta; Y) = \sum_i \log f_{\mathcal{N}}(Y_i; \mu,\Sigma)$ and maximize it
at the complete-data MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{com}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell_{\text{com}}(\theta; Y)$.
This is the gold standard MI aims to recover.

**D4 (observed-data log-likelihood and MLE).** From the observed entries alone, each row
contributes only its marginal density, giving
$\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta; Y_{\text{obs}}) = \sum_i \log f_{\mathcal{N}}(Y_{i,\text{obs}}; \mu_{O_i},\Sigma_{O_iO_i})$,
maximized at the observed-data (FIML) MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell_{\text{obs}}$.
The gap between $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ and $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ is the source of Term B.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1510</code></a>: the observed-data MLE is the maximizer of $\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})$ — "$\hat\theta$ is now the observed-data MLE, the maximizer of $l(\theta\mid Y_{obs})$".
:::

### Information matrices {#sec-information}

Each estimator's precision is governed by a Fisher information, and the two informations
differ by exactly the information the missing data would have supplied.

**D5 (complete information).** The complete-data Fisher information is
$$ I_{\text{com}}(\theta) = -\,\mathbb{E}_{Y\sim\theta}\!\big[\partial^2\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)/\partial\theta\,\partial\theta^\top\big] = \mathbb{E}\big[S_{\text{com}}(\theta)\,S_{\text{com}}(\theta)^\top\big]. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, <a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>: Fisher information $I_\theta=\mathbb{E}_\theta[\dot\ell_\theta\dot\ell_\theta^\top]$ and curvature $P_\theta\ddot\ell_\theta=-I_\theta$. MVN closed form: D15.
:::

**D6 (observed information).** The observed-data (expected) Fisher information — the
asymptotic precision of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ (Lemma L1) — is
$$ I_{\text{obs}}(\theta) = -\,\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{obs}}\sim\theta}\!\big[\partial^2\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta;Y_{\text{obs}})/\partial\theta\,\partial\theta^\top\big]. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1489</code></a>: observed information $I_o=-\partial^2\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})/\partial\theta^2$; we use its expectation, the Fisher form (@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, <a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>). For MVN, the pattern-mixture sum (`fisher_info_obs_mvn`).
:::

**D7 (missing information and the information identity).** Their difference is the missing
information, $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta) := I_{\text{com}}(\theta) - I_{\text{obs}}(\theta)$,
equivalently the additive identity
$$ I_{\text{com}}(\theta) = I_{\text{obs}}(\theta) + I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta). $$ {#eq-info-identity}
It is positive semidefinite, $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0$: missingness can only
cost information, never create it.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, missing-information principle: Schafer establishes $I_c=I_o+I_m$ *conditional on* $Y_{\text{obs}}$ via the EM decomposition $Q(\theta\mid\phi)=\ell_{\text{obs}}+H(\theta\mid\phi)$, with $I_c=-\partial^2 Q(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1483</code></a>], $I_m=-\partial^2 H(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1495</code></a>], eq (3.19) [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1501</code></a>]. Taking $\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{obs}}}$ gives the identity for the Fisher informations D5-D6 (his conditional $I_c,I_o,I_m$ average to our $I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}},I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$). $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0$ `[mc]` (min eigenvalue $\approx 0$ on the fixture, @lst-mc-psd).
:::

### The relative increase in variance (RIV) {#sec-riv}

The ratio of missing to observed information is the quantity in which the whole result is
written.

**D8 (relative increase in variance).** The matrix relative increase in variance,
$$ \mathrm{RIV}(\theta) := I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\, I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}, $$ {#eq-riv}
is Rubin's scalar $r$ in matrix form — a measure of how much missingness inflates the
variance of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ relative to the complete-data optimum. Its trace is
the scalar that drives the theorem.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source (name, scalar form).** @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2194</code></a>: the relative increase in variance due to nonresponse $r_m = (1+m^{-1})B_m/\bar U_m$; with within $\bar U_m=\sum_l U_{*l}/m$ [<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2167</code></a>], between $B_m=\sum_l(\hat Q_{*l}-\bar Q_m)^{t}(\hat Q_{*l}-\bar Q_m)/(m-1)$ [<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2173</code></a>], total $T_m=\bar U_m+(1+m^{-1})B_m$ [<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2179</code></a>].
:::

An equivalent form, used throughout: substituting $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}=I_{\text{com}}-I_{\text{obs}}$
(D7) and using $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{obs}}=I_k$,
$$ \mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}} - I_k, \qquad \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = \operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k, $$ {#eq-trriv}
so the trace appearing in the theorem is $\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Trace algebra.** @petersen2008matrix, §1.1: trace linearity $\operatorname{Tr}(A+B)=\operatorname{Tr}A+\operatorname{Tr}B$ [<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:183</code></a>], $\operatorname{tr}(I_k)=k$ from $\operatorname{Tr}(A)=\sum_i A_{ii}$ [<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:179</code></a>], and cyclicity $\operatorname{Tr}(AB)=\operatorname{Tr}(BA)$ [<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:182</code></a>].
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Identification: RIV equals Rubin's $r_\infty$
Under congenial proper MI as $M\to\infty$: $\bar U_\infty = I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$
(complete-data MLE variance) and $T_\infty = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (variance recovery;
Lemma L4), so $B_\infty = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ and
$$ r_\infty = B_\infty \bar U_\infty^{-1} = (I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1})I_{\text{com}} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}} - I_k = \mathrm{RIV}. $$
Confirmed numerically in @lst-mc-fisher.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Disambiguation — RIV is not the EM rate / "fraction of missing information"

Schafer's **fraction of missing information / EM rate matrix** is
$D = I_{\text{com}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$
[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997 eq (3.21), $D = I_c^{-1}(\hat\theta)I_m(\hat\theta)$], <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1517</code></a>.
It shares $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ with RIV but uses $I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ on the left, not
$I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$. They relate by $D = (I_k+\mathrm{RIV})^{-1}\mathrm{RIV}$ (scalar:
$\gamma = r/(1+r)$). Our headline $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}})-k$
lives specifically on the $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ side; a reader who substitutes $D$ gets the wrong quantity.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Two informations, two RIVs: expected vs sample (observed) {#sec-two-rivs}

The theorem uses the **expected** Fisher informations of D5-D6, so its
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})$
is the **expected-information RIV**. Two informations sit behind it. The
**expected (curvature) information** $-\mathbb{E}[\partial^2\ell]$ and the **score variance**
$\mathbb{E}[SS^\top]$ coincide under correct specification — the second Bartlett identity, or
*information equality* — which is why D5 writes
$I_{\text{com}}=-\mathbb{E}[\partial^2\ell]=\mathbb{E}[S_{\text{com}}S_{\text{com}}^\top]$ as one
object [@pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013, §13.6: "assuming the model is correct we get
$\mathcal{J}=\mathcal{I}$, and the formula reduces to ... the 'naive' variance"], <a href="#sec-src-pawitan"><code>:14699</code></a>. Their
inequality *under misspecification* is exactly what turns AIC's penalty $k$ into Takeuchi's
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$ (TIC/GIC)
[@pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013; @konishiGeneralisedInformationCriteria1996], <a href="#sec-src-pawitan"><code>:14707</code></a>, <a href="#sec-src-konishi"><code>:90</code></a>.

That misspecification regime is **not** ours: the analysis model is correctly specified, so
information equality holds — confirmed numerically,
$\operatorname{tr}(\hat{\mathcal{J}}\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})\approx k$ across cells
(`run_all.R` phase 6, `verification/cache/run_all-prod/phase6-info/`). What still diverges at
finite $n$ is the **expected vs observed** information: the actual sampling variance of an MLE
is better described by the *observed* information than by its expectation
[@efronAssessingAccuracyMaximum1978], <a href="#sec-src-efron"><code>:99</code></a>. Equivalently there is a second RIV — the
**sample / operational RIV**, Rubin's between/within $r_\infty$ (callout above), equal to
$\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)$
at the *realized* finite-$n$ gap variance. This is the observed-information flavor, and the
quantity the information-criterion correction (manuscript §5) actually subtracts.

The two RIVs coincide as $n\to\infty$ (Efron-Hinkley) but differ at realistic $n$: in the MVN
running example the sample RIV exceeds the expected RIV by $\sim$10% at $n\le 2000$ and the gap
closes slowly. The two deviance-bias terms attach to *different* RIVs at finite $n$ — Term A to
the expected RIV, Term B to the sample RIV — analyzed in the note after @eq-combine.

**Symbol caution.** The proof below writes $\mathcal{J}$ for the *observed information* (the
negative Hessian $-\partial^2\ell/\partial\theta\partial\theta^\top$ at the estimate), the
EM/Louis convention. This is **not** Pawitan's $\mathcal{J}$ (the score variance
$\mathbb{E}[SS^\top]$); under correct specification both reduce to $I$, so the body's
$\mathcal{J}\to_p I$ steps are unaffected.
:::

### The MI Q-function and the deviance-bias split {#sec-qfun}

We can now name the object the theorem is about, and the bias attributed to it.

**D9 (MI Q-function).** Multiple imputation does not maximize $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ directly; it
averages the complete-data log-likelihood over imputed completions. In the
infinite-imputation limit this average is
$$ \bar Q_\infty(\theta) := \lim_{M\to\infty}\frac1M\sum_{l=1}^M \ell_{\text{com}}(\theta; Y_{\text{obs}}, Y_{\text{mis}}^{(l)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}}}\!\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta)\big], $$
the EM/Meng Q-function in the proper-MI limit.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** Deterministic-FIML evaluation `barQ_fiml_analytic`
(`verification/00-setup.R`), `[mc]` equal to this construction on the fixture (@lst-mc-fiml).
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Notation clash with Rubin's pooled estimand

$\bar Q_\infty$ here is the averaged *log-likelihood*. It is **not** Rubin's
pooled point estimate $\bar Q_m = \sum_l \hat Q_{*l}/m$
[@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987], <a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2161</code></a>, which averages complete-data
*estimates*. We reserve $\bar Q_\infty$ for the averaged log-likelihood throughout.
:::

**D10–D12 (deviance-bias terms).** The theorem compares $\bar Q_\infty$ at
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ against the complete-data gold standard
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$, split at the pivot $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ into an
imputation-bias term $A$ and an estimation-mismatch term $B$ (on the log-likelihood scale;
the deviance scale doubles each):
$$
A := \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})
\quad(\text{imputation bias}),
$$
$$
B := \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})
\quad(\text{estimation mismatch}),
$$
$$
T := A + B = \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})
\quad(\text{total deviance bias}).
$$ {#eq-decomp}

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** This work; operationalized in the Monte Carlo study (`verification/`).
The theorem (@sec-derivation): $\mathbb{E}[T]=+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$,
$\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.
:::

### Congeniality {#sec-congeniality}

**D13 (congeniality).** We assume the imputation and analysis models are congenial in
Meng's sense — loosely, the imputer's posterior moments agree with the analyst's
procedure. This is what licenses the variance-recovery and Q-function identities the proof
relies on (Lemma L4); it is stated precisely as an assumption in @sec-ledger.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Definitions 1-2: posterior moments match the analyst's procedure — $[\hat\theta(Z_o),U(Z_o)]\simeq[E_f(\theta\mid Z_o),V_f(\theta\mid Z_o)]$ [eq (2.3.1), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:103</code></a>] and $[\hat\theta(Z_c),U(Z_c)]\simeq[E_f(\theta\mid Z_c),V_f(\theta\mid Z_c)]$ [eq (2.3.2), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:109</code></a>]; Definition 2 adds $f(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Z_o)=g(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Z_o,A)$ [eq (2.3.3), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:121</code></a>].
:::

### vech and the duplication matrix {#sec-vech}

Because $\theta$ carries $\Sigma$ through $\operatorname{vech}\Sigma$, the normal information
matrices below are written with the duplication matrix relating the half- and
full-vectorizations of a symmetric matrix.

**D14 (half-vectorization and duplication matrix).** For symmetric $A$ ($n\times n$),
$\operatorname{vech}(A)$ stacks the $\tfrac12 n(n+1)$ entries of its lower triangle, and the
duplication matrix $D_n$ satisfies
$$ D_n \operatorname{vech}(A) = \operatorname{vec} A, \qquad \operatorname{vech}(A) = (D_n'D_n)^{-1}D_n'\operatorname{vec} A. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019, Ch 3 §15: duplication matrix $D_n\operatorname{vech}(A)=\operatorname{vec}A$ (eq (23)) [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1658</code></a>], $\operatorname{vech}$ definition [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1652</code></a>], inverse identity $(D_n'(A\otimes A)D_n)^{-1}=(D_n'D_n)^{-1}D_n'(A^{-1}\otimes A^{-1})D_n(D_n'D_n)^{-1}$ [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1692</code></a>].
:::

### MVN instantiation of the information matrices {#sec-mvn-info}

For the normal model the complete information has a closed form — the one the verification
in @sec-verify computes with.

**D15 (closed-form MVN information).** On $\theta=(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ the
complete information is block-diagonal,
$$ I_{\text{com}}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} N\Sigma^{-1} & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{N}{2}D_p'(\Sigma^{-1}\otimes\Sigma^{-1})D_p \end{pmatrix}. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019, MVN ML section: information for $\mu$ and $\operatorname{vech}(\Omega)$ as $n\Omega^{-1}$ and $\tfrac{n}{2}D_m'(\Omega^{-1}\otimes\Omega^{-1})D_m$ [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1800</code></a>-<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1813</code></a>] ($\Omega\!\leftrightarrow\!\Sigma$, $m\!\leftrightarrow\!p$, $n\!\leftrightarrow\!N$). $I_{\text{obs}}$ is the pattern-mixture sum of per-pattern marginal informations (`fisher_info_obs_mvn`, `verification/00-setup.R`); `[mc]` against this closed form in @lst-mc-fisher.
:::

---

## Assumptions and lemmas {#sec-ledger}

This section collects what the proof rests on but does not itself establish: the
regularity, ignorability, congeniality, and properness conditions (R1–R7); the standard
matrix and expectation identities; and four asymptotic lemmas (L1–L4). Each labeled item
is cited by the proof in @sec-derivation. As in @sec-notation, provenance — source,
equation or theorem number, and line reference, with any `[mc]`/`[derived]` status — sits
in the margin.

### Regularity, congeniality, and properness {#sec-reg}

Seven conditions are in force throughout, and each is simply the hypothesis under which
some later step is exact to leading order. R1–R2 are the standard likelihood-asymptotics
regularity that makes the Fisher informations and the MLE expansions of @sec-lem
well-defined. R3 makes the missingness ignorable, so the mechanism drops out of the
likelihood (D2). R4–R7 are the multiple-imputation conditions — congeniality, properness,
self-efficiency, and the infinite-imputation idealization — that let the imputation limit
recover the observed-data variance (Lemma L4) and that fix
$\operatorname{Cov}(\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ in Term A. None is exotic.

**R1 (regular model).** The complete-data model is differentiable in quadratic mean, with
Lipschitz log-density and a consistent MLE; differentiation and integration interchange, so
$P_\theta\dot\ell_\theta=0$ (scores are mean-zero) and $P_\theta\ddot\ell_\theta=-I_\theta$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, Thm 5.39: regularity conditions [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:7388</code></a>] and score identities [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>]; @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1480</code></a>: "sufficient regularity to interchange differentiation and integration".
:::

**R2 (nonsingular information).** Both informations are positive definite,
$I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}}\succ 0$, so the inverses in RIV and in the variance formulas
below exist.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, §5.5: nonsingular Fisher information $I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}}\succ 0$ (see @sec-src-vaart).
:::

**R3 (ignorability).** The data are missing at random, with the missingness parameters
distinct from $\theta$, so the missingness mechanism need not be modeled (D2).

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinInferenceMissingData1976b, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1976"><code>:39</code></a> and @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, <a href="#sec-src-little"><code>:3263</code></a>: ignorability (MAR + distinct parameters).
:::

**R4 (congeniality).** The imputation and analysis models are congenial in Meng's sense
(D13) — the imputer's posterior moments agree with the analyst's procedure.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Defs 1-2: congeniality [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:99</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:118</code></a>].
:::

**R5 (properness).** The imputation is second-moment proper in Rubin's sense: its posterior
draws carry the correct first two moments, so the between-imputation variance behaves as the
combining rules assume.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987 and @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, cond. (c), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:506</code></a>: second-moment proper imputation.
:::

**R6 (self-efficiency).** The analyst's estimator is self-efficient: no convex combination
of the observed- and complete-data estimators beats the complete-data one, i.e. no $\lambda$
makes $\operatorname{MSE}(\lambda\hat\theta(W_o)+(1-\lambda)\hat\theta(W_c))<\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta(W_c))$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Def 5: self-efficiency [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:450</code></a>], cond. (a) [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:504</code></a>].
:::

**R7 ($M\to\infty$).** Infinite-imputation idealization ($\bar Q_\infty,T_\infty$);
finite-$M$ corrections are Rubin's.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987, §4: infinite-imputation idealization (see @sec-src-rubin1987).
:::

### Matrix and expectation identities {#sec-alg}

The proof uses only standard identities, named at the point of use and reproduced verbatim
with line references in @sec-sources:

- **Trace.** Linearity and cyclicity, and $\operatorname{tr} I_k = k$
  [@petersen2008matrix].
- **vec / Kronecker.** $\operatorname{vec}(AXB)=(B^\top\!\otimes A)\operatorname{vec}X$ and
  $\operatorname{Tr}(A^\top B)=\operatorname{vec}(A)^\top\operatorname{vec}(B)$
  [@petersen2008matrix].
- **Symmetry.** The information matrices are symmetric, with $(A^{-1})^\top=(A^\top)^{-1}$.
- **Duplication matrix.** The identities of @sec-vech, including
  $(D_n'(A\otimes A)D_n)^{-1}=(D_n'D_n)^{-1}D_n'(A^{-1}\!\otimes A^{-1})D_n(D_n'D_n)^{-1}$
  [@magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019].
- **Expectation.** Linearity, iterated expectation
  $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}(X\mid Y)]=\mathbb{E}X$, the $L^2$-projection characterization of
  conditional expectation, and the quadratic-form identity
  $\mathbb{E}[v^\top M v]=\operatorname{tr}(M\,\mathbb{E}[vv^\top])$
  [@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a].
- **Second-order Taylor expansion** [@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a].
- **Gaussian conditional (Schur complement).** For the normal Q-function,
  $\Sigma_{11\cdot 2}=\Sigma_{11}-\Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21}$ with conditional
  mean $\mu^{(1)}+\Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}(x^{(2)}-\mu^{(2)})$
  [@andersonIntroductionMultivariateStatistical2003].

### Asymptotic lemmas {#sec-lem}

Four facts about the estimators and the infinite-imputation limit supply the asymptotic
inputs the proof draws on.

**L1 (MLE asymptotic linearity and normality).** Under R1-R2,
$$ \sqrt{n}(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt n}\textstyle\sum_i I_{\theta_0}^{-1}\dot\ell_{\theta_0}(X_i)+o_P(1)\;\rightsquigarrow\;N(0,I_{\theta_0}^{-1}), $$
hence $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$, $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, Thm 5.39: linear expansion [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:7388</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:7393</code></a>], covariance $I_\theta^{-1}$ [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:4170</code></a>].
:::

**L3 (Fisher/Louis score identity).** Differentiating the loglik
decomposition $\ell(\theta\mid Y)=\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})+\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)+c$
once and taking $\mathbb{E}[\cdot\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$ (conditional score mean-zero, R1) gives
$U_{\text{obs}}=\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{com}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$; hence by A8,
$\operatorname{Cov}(U_{\text{obs}},U_{\text{com}})=\operatorname{Var}(U_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, loglik decomposition: $\ell(\theta\mid Y)=\ell_{\text{obs}}+\log P$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:944</code></a>], $Q/H$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:951</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:964</code></a>], 2nd-derivative analog (3.19) [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1501</code></a>]; @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, <a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>: score-mean-zero.
:::

**L2 (variance of the estimator gap).**
$$ \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}}) = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}. $$ {#eq-varGap}
*Proof.* By L1, $\hat\theta_\bullet-\theta_0\approx I_\bullet^{-1}U_\bullet$; by L3,
$\operatorname{Cov}(U_{\text{obs}},U_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}$, so
$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}},\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{obs}}I_{\text{com}}^{-1}=I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$;
then $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}+I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$.
The variance is of the *gap*, not of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ alone — the distinction that
keeps Term B at $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ rather than a spurious $-\tfrac12 k$.
This identity is exact only to leading order: it rests on
$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}},\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ (via L3),
which holds asymptotically. At finite $n$ the realized gap variance is *larger*, so Term B in
fact tracks the **sample** RIV rather than the expected one — see the finite-$n$ note after
@eq-combine and the two-RIV callout in @sec-riv.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** `[derived]` from L1, L3, A8; verified symbolically (Tier B, @lst-mc-tierb).
:::

**L4 (MI variance recovery).** Under R4-R7, $\bar\theta_\infty$ is consistent
and $T_\infty=\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$. (Anchors the D8 RIV
identification.)

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Lemma 3 [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:486</code></a>] and Main result [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:503</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:511</code></a>]: variance recovery; also @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987, §4 (see @sec-src-rubin1987).
:::

## Main result and proof {#sec-derivation}

Each step names the assumptions and lemmas of @sec-ledger that it invokes; a margin `[mc]`
marks a step checked symbolically in @sec-verify. Let $\theta_0$ denote the data-generating
parameter and $\tilde\phi$ the imputation parameter — the symbol $\phi$ rather than
$\theta$ marks it as the imputer's, distinct from the analysis parameter even where the two
spaces coincide under congeniality, and the tilde marks a posterior *draw* (which collapses
to the point value $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ under FIML). We write $S$ for the score and
$\mathcal{J}$ for the observed information (the negative Hessian at the estimate) — *not* the
score-variance $\mathcal{J}$ of @pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013 (<a href="#sec-src-pawitan"><code>:14618</code></a>); under correct
specification both reduce to the expected information $I$ (see the two-RIV callout in
@sec-riv), so the $\mathcal{J}\to_p I$ steps below are unaffected.

### Theorem {#sec-thm}

**Theorem (Q-function deviance bias).** Under R1-R7, to leading order
$$ \mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}};Y)\big] = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + O(n^{-1}), $$ {#eq-theorem}
decomposing (D10-D12) as $\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$ (imputation
bias) and $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$ (estimation
mismatch). The deviance-scale statement multiplies by 2.

Here $\mathrm{RIV}$ is the **expected-information** RIV (D8). The statement is asymptotic: the
leading constants are exact, but at finite $n$ Term A and Term B calibrate to *different*
RIVs (expected vs sample/observed), so the approach to the leading constants is governed by
the Efron–Hinkley expected-vs-sample gap rather than a uniform $O(n^{-1})$ — see the two-RIV
callout (@sec-riv) and the finite-$n$ note after @eq-combine.

### Proof strategy {#sec-strategy}

The proof pivots on the observed-data estimator $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, splitting the
total bias into the two terms of @eq-decomp,
$$
T = \underbrace{\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\big]}_{A:\ \text{imputation bias}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big]}_{B:\ \text{estimation mismatch}},
$$
and evaluating each in expectation to leading order.

*Term B* is elementary. Both quantities are complete-data log-likelihoods, so their
difference is a second-order Taylor expansion of $\ell_{\text{com}}$ about its maximizer
$\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$; its expectation is
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)$.
The variance of the estimator *gap* is $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ (the
variance-of-the-gap lemma below), and the trace collapses to
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The single trap — substituting
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ for the variance of the gap — would produce a
spurious $-\tfrac12 k$; the lemma is stated precisely to forestall it.

*Term A* is the delicate half. The missing-data log-density is now averaged over
completions drawn from the imputation model rather than the truth, and a second-order
expansion in the imputation parameter $\tilde\phi$ yields three pieces. The leading
cross-term gives $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$; the remaining two — an entropy-gradient
term and a cross-entropy-curvature term, each $O(1)$ — cancel to $O(n^{-1})$. That
cancellation is the subtle step (an earlier shortcut wrongly set one piece to zero), and
we isolate and prove it as a lemma inside the Term A argument.

Summing, $\mathbb{E}[T] = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) - \tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.

### Term B $=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (estimation mismatch) {#sec-termB}

This term is elementary and exact to leading order.

**B1.** $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}=\arg\max_\theta\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)$ [D3], so
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\ge\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ and $B\le 0$.

**B2.** A second-order Taylor expansion of $\ell_{\text{com}}$ about its maximizer
$\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, whose first-order term vanishes since $S_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=0$ [D3]:
$$ B = -\tfrac12(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})^\top \mathcal{J}_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}}) + O_p(n^{-3/2}). $$

**B3.** Take $\mathbb{E}$; replace $\mathcal{J}_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\to_p I_{\text{com}}$ [R1, R2];
apply the quadratic-form identity $a^\top Ba=\operatorname{tr}(Baa^\top)$; both estimators are
asymptotically unbiased for $\theta_0$, so $\mathbb{E}[\cdot\,(\cdot)^\top]$ is the variance of the gap:
$$ \mathbb{E}[B] = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)+O(n^{-3/2}). $$

**B4.** Substitute L2, then reduce the trace:
$$ \mathbb{E}[B] = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1})\big)\ \text{[L2]}\ = -\tfrac12\big(\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}})-k\big)\ \text{(trace algebra)}\ = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})\ \text{[D8]}. $$ {#eq-termB}

::: {.column-margin}
`[mc]` @lst-mc-termb (`B + tr(RIV)/2 == 0`).
:::

**Finite-$n$ (which RIV).** Term B consumes the variance of the estimator *gap* (L2). That
variance's leading identity $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ is finite-sample
*inflated*, so $\mathbb{E}[B]$ in fact tracks $-\tfrac12$ the **sample / observed** RIV
$\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)$,
the larger of the two (@sec-riv). Empirically
$\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{com}}\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\text{gap}))\approx 6.0$ vs the
expected $5.5$ at $n=2000$ (`phase6-info/`); the gap closes only as $n\to\infty$.

### Term A $=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (imputation bias) {#sec-termA}

**A1.** Factor the complete-data log-likelihood
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)=\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta;Y_{\text{obs}})+\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta)$,
$\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta):=\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$.
The $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ parts cancel in $A$ (D9, D10), leaving

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, eq (3.2): complete-data factorization [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:944</code></a>].
:::
$$ A = \mathbb{E}\big[\,\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\tilde\phi}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})] - \ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\,\big]. $$

**A2.** By iterated expectation over the true completion $Y_{\text{mis}}\sim\theta_0$,
$$ A = \mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{obs}}}\big[\,\mathbb{E}_{\tilde\phi}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})] - \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})]\,\big], $$
the discrepancy between averaging the missing-data log-density under $\tilde\phi$ versus the truth $\theta_0$.

**A3.** Second-order Taylor in $\tilde\phi$ about $\theta_0$, using the score-function
identity $\partial_\phi\mathbb{E}_\phi[g]\big|_{\theta_0}=\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[g\,S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}]$
[R1, differentiate under the integral] and Bartlett
$\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}^\top]=I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$
[R1, D7], then expanding $\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ about $\theta_0$, gives three pieces:
$$ A = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)^\top\alpha]}_{(A)} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)^\top I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\theta_0)]}_{(B)\ \text{main}} + \underbrace{\tfrac12\mathbb{E}[(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)^\top H_{\phi\phi}(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)]}_{(C)}. $$

::: {.callout-warning}
## $\alpha$ is not zero (do not invoke Gibbs)
$\alpha = \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\,S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)] = \operatorname{Cov}_{\theta_0}(\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}},S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}) = -\mathcal{E}'(\theta_0)$,
the gradient of the conditional entropy — generally nonzero (e.g. $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$:
$\alpha_\mu=0$, $\alpha_{\sigma^2}=-n_{\text{mis}}/2\sigma^2\neq 0$). Gibbs' inequality
concerns varying the *evaluation* point of $\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\log p_\theta]$; $\alpha$
varies the *sampling* distribution. The two are different functions — an earlier
shortcut that set $\alpha=0$ was wrong (v4.1 correction).
:::

**A4 (cancellation).** Pieces $(A)$ and $(C)$ are each $O(1)$ but their leading
contributions cancel — $(A)$ pairs the bias of $\tilde\phi$ with the entropy
gradient, $(C)$ the curvature of the conditional cross-entropy with $\operatorname{Var}(\tilde\phi)$,
linked through the information geometry of $P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$:
$$ (A)+(C) = O(n^{-1}). $$
The mechanism and its closed-form verification are spelled out in the cancellation
lemma immediately below. To leading order only the main piece $(B)$ survives:
$A = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\operatorname{Cov}(\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\big)+O(n^{-1})$.

::: {.column-margin}
`[derived]` Mechanism + two closed-form families in the lemma below; closed forms
computed in the companion MI-IC derivation (v4, Steps 6-7) and reproduced here so this
document is self-contained. The finite-$n$ behaviour of $A$ and $B$ — each attaching to a
*different* RIV (expected vs sample) — is analyzed in the note after @eq-combine.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Lemma (the $(A)+(C)$ cancellation)

Let $f_0(\phi):=\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\sim\phi}\big[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\big]$ be the
conditional **cross-entropy** — the expected missing-data log-density evaluated at the
truth $\theta_0$, over completions drawn from $\phi$. With $\delta:=\tilde\phi-\theta_0$,
pieces $(A)$ and $(C)$ are exactly the first- and second-order terms of the Taylor
expansion of $f_0$ about $\theta_0$:
$$ (A)+(C) = \mathbb{E}[\delta^\top\nabla f_0(\theta_0)] + \tfrac12\mathbb{E}[\delta^\top\nabla^2 f_0(\theta_0)\delta] + O(n^{-3/2}), $$
with $\nabla f_0(\theta_0)=\alpha$ (the entropy gradient of A3) and
$\nabla^2 f_0(\theta_0)=H_{\phi\phi}$.

**Curvature identity** [R1]. Differentiating
$\nabla f_0(\phi)=\mathbb{E}_\phi[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\,S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\phi)]$
once more under the integral (R1) and using $\nabla_\phi S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}=-\mathcal{J}_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$:
$$ H_{\phi\phi} = \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}\!\big[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\,(S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}^\top - \mathcal{J}_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})\big],\qquad \mathcal{J}_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}=-\nabla^2_\phi\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\phi). $$

**Order counting.** Under proper MI centered at $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ [R5, L1],
$\mathbb{E}[\delta]=\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\theta_0]=O(n^{-1})$ (MLE bias) and
$\operatorname{Var}(\delta)=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}=O(n^{-1})$, while $\alpha=O(n)$ and
$H_{\phi\phi}=O(n)$ (both extensive in $n_{\text{mis}}$). Hence
$$ (A)=\mathbb{E}[\delta]^\top\alpha = O(1),\qquad (C)=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(H_{\phi\phi}\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big)+O(n^{-1}) = O(1) $$
are two $O(1)$ pieces of opposite sign whose leading parts cancel through the structural
pairing of the entropy gradient $\alpha=-\mathcal{E}'(\theta_0)$ and the cross-entropy
curvature $H_{\phi\phi}$ over $P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$.

**Closed-form verification** (MLE imputation $\tilde\phi=\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$;
$n_{\text{obs}}$ observed, $n_{\text{mis}}$ missing):

| Family | $(A)$ | $(C)=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(H_{\phi\phi}I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})$ | $(A)+(C)$ | Term A |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | $+\,n_{\text{mis}}/(2n_{\text{obs}})$ | $-\,n_{\text{mis}}/(2n_{\text{obs}})$ | $0$ (exact) | $n_{\text{mis}}/(n_{\text{obs}}-2)=\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$ |
| $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$ | $+\,n_{\text{mis}}/(n_{\text{obs}}-1)$ | $-\,n_{\text{mis}}/n_{\text{obs}}$ | $n_{\text{mis}}/[n_{\text{obs}}(n_{\text{obs}}-1)]=O(n^{-1})$ | $n_{\text{mis}}/(n_{\text{obs}}-1)=\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$ |

**Scope (honest statement).** The two families are *exact closed-form* checks; the
curvature and order-counting identities above supply the general mechanism, so the
cancellation is **asserted** for any regular conditional family $P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$
[R1: differentiation under the integral, Bartlett identities] — it is **not** proved here
as a closed theorem at that generality. Two pieces of independent support: (i) the FIML
$A$ matches the R code to machine precision and the W1 cache gives
$\mathbb{E}[A]\approx\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ against the expected-information RIV
(@lst-mc-fiml, @lst-mc-mcse); (ii) the high-$R$ diagnostic (`run_all.R` phase 6) confirms this
at $n\le 2000$, with the finite-$n$ behaviour of $A$, $B$, $T$ — and why they attach to
different RIVs — analyzed in the note after @eq-combine.
:::

**A5.** Under congenial proper imputation the imputation parameter satisfies
$\tilde\phi=\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}+\Delta\tilde\phi$ with $\operatorname{Cov}(\Delta\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=0$ [R4, R5],
so $\operatorname{Cov}(\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ [L1].
Hence
$$ \mathbb{E}[A] = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big) + O(n^{-1}) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + O(n^{-1})\ \text{[D7, D8]}. $$ {#eq-termA}

::: {.column-margin}
`[mc]` @lst-mc-terma (`A - tr(RIV) == 0`) for the trace
identity; the MVN FIML instantiation of $\bar Q_\infty$ matches the R code to machine
precision (@lst-mc-fiml).
:::

**Finite-$n$ (which RIV).** Term A consumes
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (L1), which the observed
information tracks well even at finite $n$
($\operatorname{tr}(\hat I_{\text{obs}}\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}))\approx k$),
so $\mathbb{E}[A]$ tracks the **expected** RIV — unlike Term B, whose gap variance is inflated
(@sec-riv; note after @eq-combine).

### Combine: $T = A + B = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ {#sec-combine}

$$ \mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}[A]+\mathbb{E}[B] = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) - \tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + O(n^{-1}) $$ {#eq-combine}
[D10-D12], combining the Term A (@eq-termA) and Term B (@eq-termB) results. $\qquad\blacksquare$

::: {.column-margin}
`[mc]` @lst-mc-combine (`T - tr(RIV)/2 == 0`).
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Leading order vs finite-$n$: the two terms attach to different RIVs
$\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
hold *to leading order* against the **expected-information** RIV (D5-D6, D8). At finite $n$ the
two terms calibrate to *different* RIVs (@sec-riv):

- **Term A** is governed by $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (L1),
  which the observed information tracks well even at finite $n$, so $A$ tracks the **expected** RIV.
- **Term B** is governed by $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$ (L2),
  whose asymptotic identity $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ is finite-sample *inflated*,
  so $B$ tracks the larger **sample / observed** RIV (Efron-Hinkley,
  @efronAssessingAccuracyMaximum1978, <a href="#sec-src-efron"><code>:113</code></a>).

Hence
$\mathbb{E}[T]-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})_{\text{exp}}
=\big(\mathbb{E}[A]-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})_{\text{exp}}\big)
-\tfrac12\big(\mathrm{RIV}_{\text{samp}}-\mathrm{RIV}_{\text{exp}}\big)$:
at finite $n$, $T$ sits *below* $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ by about half the
expected-vs-sample gap, vanishing only as that (Efron-Hinkley) gap closes — empirically
**slowly**, not as a clean $O(n^{-1})$. Each term is $+O(n^{-1})$ against *its own* RIV
(e.g. $\mathbb{E}[B]+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})_{\text{samp}}\to0$ cleanly); it is
the *cross-discrepancy* between the two RIVs that is slow. The earlier reading — that the
per-term residuals cancel so $T$ holds — was a Monte-Carlo artifact of insufficient precision,
corrected by the high-$R$ runs (`run_all.R` phase 6; `todo/008`).

Three layers of evidence: this proof (the algebra), the inline symbolic verification
(@sec-verify), and the pre-registered + diagnostic Monte Carlo study (`verification/`,
including the phase-6 information diagnostic).
:::

---

## Appendix A — Verbatim source passages {#sec-sources}

Consolidated provenance audit, grouped by reference. Each entry reproduces — verbatim
from the `literature/<citekey>.md` Mathpix conversions — the specific passages the
derivation invokes, annotated with the source's own equation/theorem number and the
`literature/<citekey>.md` line. The margin provenance blocks in the preceding sections keep a short
snippet as a re-pagination-stable anchor and link here (`@sec-src-<key>`) for the full
text.

**Fair use and post-review deletion.** These excerpts are reproduced for the transparency
audit that JAIGP norms call for, so a reviewer can check each cited claim against its source
without leaving the document. To keep each quotation interpretable we include a little
surrounding context — the lead-in, the cited statement, and the following clause.
**These verbatim passages are temporary: they will be deleted once the blinded peer-review
process concludes**, leaving only the equation/theorem numbers and the
`literature/<citekey>.md` line references. Should the venue or a reviewer object to verbatim
reproduction at any earlier point, trim each entry to its number and line reference and drop
the quoted text.

### Schafer (1997) — Analysis of Incomplete Multivariate Data {#sec-src-schafer}

[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997]. Invoked by D1, D4, D6, D7, R1, L3, Term A step A1.

**Complete-data likelihood factorization, eq (3.2)** [`:941`–`:947`].

> Viewing each term in (3.1) as a function of $\theta$, it follows that
> $l(\theta \mid Y)=l(\theta \mid Y_{obs})+\log P(Y_{mis} \mid Y_{obs}, \theta)+c$, where
> $l(\theta/Y)=\log P(Y \mid \theta)$ denotes the complete-data log likelihood,
> $l(\theta/Y_{obs})$ … the observed-data loglikelihood, and $c$ an arbitrary constant. The
> term $P(Y_{mis} \mid Y_{obs}, \theta)$, which we shall call the predictive distribution of
> the missing data given $\theta$, plays a central role in EM because it captures the
> interdependence between $Y_{mis}$ and $\theta$.

**EM decomposition of the Q-function** [`:949`–`:964`].

> Because $Y_{mis}$ is unknown we cannot calculate the second term on the right-hand side of
> (3.2), so instead we take the average of (3.2) over the predictive distribution
> $P(Y_{mis} \mid Y_{obs}, \theta^{(t)})$ … This averaging yields
> $Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})=l(\theta \mid Y_{obs})+H(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})+c$, where
> $Q(\theta\mid\theta^{(t)})=\int l(\theta\mid Y)\,P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta^{(t)})\,dY_{mis}$
> and $H(\theta\mid\theta^{(t)})=\int \log P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta)\,P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta^{(t)})\,dY_{mis}$.

**Missing-information principle and the information identity, eq (3.19)** [`:1480`–`:1504`].

> Taking the expectation of this over $P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta)$, we obtain a
> fundamental relationship: the complete information is equal to the observed information
> plus the missing information. This relationship, called the missing information principle
> by Orchard and Woodbury (1972) … Assuming sufficient regularity to interchange the order
> of differentiation and integration, we can write the complete information as
> $I_c(\theta)=-\partial^2 Q(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$, the observed information as
> $I_o(\theta)=-\partial^2 l(\theta\mid Y_{obs})/\partial\theta^2$, and the missing
> information as $I_m(\theta)=-\partial^2 H(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$, so that
> $I_c(\theta)=I_o(\theta)+I_m(\theta)$ (3.19). Note that each quantity in (3.19) is a
> function of $Y_{obs}$ although this fact has been suppressed in the notation.

**Observed-data MLE** [`:1504`–`:1510`].

> A natural large-sample estimate of the covariance matrix with incomplete data is
> $I_o^{-1}(\hat\theta)$ … where $\hat\theta$ is now the observed-data MLE, the maximizer of
> $l(\theta\mid Y_{obs})$.

**EM rate matrix (fraction of missing information), eq (3.21)** [`:1514`–`:1520`] — cited
for the D8 disambiguation; this is *not* RIV.

> Dempster, Laird and Rubin (1977) established an important connection between these
> information quantities and $D$ … the asymptotic rate matrix of EM … they showed that
> $D=I_c^{-1}(\hat\theta)\, I_m(\hat\theta)$ (3.21) … for a scalar parameter (3.21) implies
> that $D$ is the ratio of the missing information to the complete information. For brevity
> we will call this ratio the fraction of missing information.

### Magnus & Neudecker (2019) — Matrix Differential Calculus {#sec-src-magnus}

[@magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019]. Invoked by D14, D15, and @sec-alg.

**Half-vectorization** [`:1652`] (here for $3\times3$ symmetric $A$).

> $\operatorname{vech}(A)=\left(a_{11}, a_{21}, a_{31}, a_{22}, a_{32}, a_{33}\right)^{\prime}$

**Duplication matrix, eq (23)** [`:1658`], and its inverse [`:1664`].

> $D_{n} \operatorname{vech}(A)=\operatorname{vec} A \quad\left(A=A^{\prime}\right)$

> $\operatorname{vech}(A)=\left(D_{n}^{\prime} D_{n}\right)^{-1} D_{n}^{\prime} \operatorname{vec} A \quad\left(A=A^{\prime}\right)$

**Inverse of $D_n'(A\otimes A)D_n$** [`:1692`].

> $\left(D_{n}^{\prime}(A \otimes A) D_{n}\right)^{-1}=\left(D_{n}^{\prime} D_{n}\right)^{-1} D_{n}^{\prime}\left(A^{-1} \otimes A^{-1}\right) D_{n}\left(D_{n}^{\prime} D_{n}\right)^{-1}$

**MVN information matrix for $(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Omega)$** [`:1804`-`:1811`]
($\Omega\!\leftrightarrow\!\Sigma$, $m\!\leftrightarrow\!p$, $n\!\leftrightarrow\!N$ in our D15).

> $\mathcal{F}=n\begin{pmatrix}\Omega^{-1} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{2} D_{m}^{\prime}\left(\Omega^{-1} \otimes \Omega^{-1}\right) D_{m}\end{pmatrix}$

### van der Vaart (1998) — Asymptotic Statistics {#sec-src-vaart}

[@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a]. Invoked by D5, D6, R1, R2, L1, L3, and @sec-alg.

**Score identities** [`:2320`].

> "$P_{\theta} \dot{\ell}_{\theta}=0$ (scores have mean zero), and $P_{\theta} \ddot{\ell}_{\theta}=-I_{\theta}$ (the curvature of the likelihood is equal to minus the Fisher information)."

**MLE asymptotic normality** [`:2325`].

> $\sqrt{n}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right) \rightsquigarrow N\left(0, I_{\theta}^{-1}\right)$

**MLE linear (influence-function) expansion, Theorem 5.39** [`:4170`, `:7388`-`:7393`];
the joint full/null-restricted form at `:7392`-`:7393` underpins L3's score covariance.

> $\sqrt{n}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{\theta}^{-1} \dot{\ell}_{\theta}\left(X_{i}\right)+o_{P_{\theta}}(1)$

**Conditional expectation as $L^2$ projection (Ch 11)** [`:5161`, `:5170`].

> (line 5161) "the conditional expectation is the unique measurable function $E(X \mid Y)$ of $Y$ that satisfies the orthogonality relation $\mathrm{E}(X-\mathrm{E}(X \mid Y)) g(Y)=0$"; (line 5170, Example 11.3) "$\mathrm{EX}=E E(X \mid Y)$ ... 'the expectation of a conditional expectation is the expectation.'"

### Meng (1994) — Multiple-Imputation Inferences with Uncongenial Sources of Input {#sec-src-meng}

[@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a]. Invoked by D1, D13, R4, R5, R6, L4.
Complete data $Z_{c}=\{X, Y_{inc}, I\}$, observed data $Z_{o}=\{X, Y_{obs}, I, R_{inc}\}$ [`:95`].

**Definition 1 (congeniality), eqs (2.3.1)-(2.3.2)** [`:102`-`:110`].

> $\left[\widehat{\theta}\left(Z_{o}\right), U\left(Z_{o}\right)\right] \simeq\left[E_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{o}\right], V_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{o}\right]\right]$ &nbsp; (2.3.1)

> $\left[\widehat{\theta}\left(Z_{c}\right), U\left(Z_{c}\right)\right] \simeq\left[E_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{c}\right], V_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{c}\right]\right]$ &nbsp; (2.3.2)

**Definition 2 (congeniality to an imputation model), eq (2.3.3)** [`:120`].

> $f\left(Y_{mis} \mid Z_{o}\right)=g\left(Y_{mis} \mid Z_{o}, A\right)$

**Definition 5 (self-efficiency)** [`:450`].

> "A statistical estimation procedure $\widehat{\theta}(\cdot)$ ... is said to be self-efficient ... if there is no $\lambda \in(-\infty, \infty)$ such that the mean-squared error of $\lambda \hat{\theta}\left(W_{o}\right)+(1-\lambda) \hat{\theta}\left(W_{c}\right)$ is less than that of $\widehat{\theta}\left(W_{c}\right)$."

**Lemma 3 and Main result (variance recovery / efficiency)** [`:486`, `:503`-`:511`]; conditions
(a) self-efficiency and (c) "second-moment proper" imputation [`:504`, `:506`].

> "(i) The repeated-imputation estimator is consistent for $\theta$, and is at least as efficient as the analyst's incomplete-data estimator."

### Rubin (1987) — Multiple Imputation for Nonresponse in Surveys {#sec-src-rubin1987}

[@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. Invoked by D8, R5, R7, L4, and the D9 notation-clash callout.

**Pooled estimate and the variance components** [`:2161`, `:2167`, `:2173`, `:2179`].

> $\bar{Q}_{m}=\sum_{l=1}^{m} \hat{Q}_{* l} / m$ ; &nbsp; $\bar{U}_{m}=\sum_{l=1}^{m} U_{* l} / m$ ; &nbsp; $B_{m}=\sum_{l=1}^{m}\left(\hat{Q}_{* l}-\bar{Q}_{m}\right)^{t}\left(\hat{Q}_{* l}-\bar{Q}_{m}\right) /(m-1)$ ; &nbsp; $T_{m}=\bar{U}_{m}+\left(1+m^{-1}\right) B_{m}$

**Relative increase in variance due to nonresponse** [`:2194`] (named at `:2191`).

> $r_{m}=\left(1+m^{-1}\right) B_{m} / \bar{U}_{m}$

### Rubin (1976) — Inference and Missing Data {#sec-src-rubin1976}

[@rubinInferenceMissingData1976b]. Invoked by D2, R3 (ignorability).

**Ignorability for likelihood / Bayesian inference (abstract)** [`:39`].

> "When making direct-likelihood or Bayesian inferences about $\theta$, it is appropriate to ignore the process that causes missing data if the missing data are missing at random and the parameter of the missing data process is 'distinct' from $\theta$."

### Little & Rubin (2019) — Statistical Analysis with Missing Data, 3rd ed. {#sec-src-little}

[@littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019]. Invoked by D1, D2, R3.

**MAR definition** [`:438`-`:444`].

> "missingness depends on $y_{i}$ only through the observed components $y_{(0)i}$ ... The missingness mechanism is then called missing at random (MAR)."

**Ignorability — Definition 6.4 and Theorem 6.1A(a)** [`:3263`, `:3277`].

> (Def 6.4) "The missingness mechanism is called ignorable if inferences for $\theta$ based on the ignorable likelihood ... are the same as inferences ... based on the full likelihood"; (Thm 6.1A(a), parameter distinctness) "the joint parameter space $(\theta, \psi)$ ... is the product of the parameter space $\Omega_{\theta}$ of $\theta$ and the parameter space $\Omega_{\psi}$ of $\psi$."

### Anderson (2003) — An Introduction to Multivariate Statistical Analysis {#sec-src-anderson}

[@andersonIntroductionMultivariateStatistical2003]. Invoked by @sec-alg (Gaussian conditional / Schur complement) and Term A's normal Q-function.

**Conditional covariance (Schur complement)** [`:1582`].

> $\Sigma_{11 \cdot 2}=\Sigma_{11}-\Sigma_{12} \Sigma_{22}^{-1} \Sigma_{21}$

**Conditional mean** [`:1598`]; the $q$-variate normal conditional density $f(x^{(1)} \mid x^{(2)})$ has this mean and covariance $\Sigma_{11\cdot2}$ [`:1595`].

> $\mathscr{E}\left(X^{(1)} \mid x^{(2)}\right)=\mu^{(1)}+\Sigma_{12} \Sigma_{22}^{-1}\left(x^{(2)}-\mu^{(2)}\right)$

### Petersen & Pedersen — The Matrix Cookbook {#sec-src-petersen}

[@petersen2008matrix]. Invoked by @sec-alg (trace and vec identities) and the @eq-trriv trace reduction.

**§1.1 Trace** [`:177`-`:187`]. In our Mathpix conversion this section is a single
unnumbered aligned display, so the Cookbook's own inline equation numbers are **not**
recoverable from the file; these trace identities are therefore cited by section + line.

> $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A})=\sum_{i} A_{ii}$ [`:179`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A}^{T})$ [`:181`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A B})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{B A})$ [`:182`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{B})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A})+\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{B})$ [`:183`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A B C})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{B C A})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{C A B})$ [`:184`]

**§10.2.2 vec-operator, eqs (520)-(521)** [`:3345`-`:3346`]. Equation numbers confirmed
in-file: the vec rule is cited as "Eq 520" at `:3692`, and "Eq. 524" closes the same
numbered block at `:3353`.

> $\operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{A X B})=\left(\mathbf{B}^{T} \otimes \mathbf{A}\right) \operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{X})$ &nbsp; (520)

> $\operatorname{Tr}\left(\mathbf{A}^{T} \mathbf{B}\right)=\operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{A})^{T} \operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{B})$ &nbsp; (521)

### Efron & Hinkley (1978) — Observed versus Expected Fisher Information {#sec-src-efron}

[@efronAssessingAccuracyMaximum1978]. Invoked by the two-RIV callout in @sec-riv and the finite-$n$ note after
@eq-combine: the *observed* information, not its expectation, tracks the finite-sample variance
of the MLE — the basis for the **sample/observed** RIV that Term B attaches to.

**Observed vs expected Fisher information** [`:99`].

> "The quantity $I(x)$ is aptly called the observed Fisher information by some writers, as distinguished from $\mathscr{I}_{\theta}$, the expected Fisher information. This last name is useful even though $E\left(\mathscr{J}_{\theta}\right) \neq \mathscr{I}_{\theta}$ in general."

**The MLE's variance is the reciprocal of the *observed* information** [`:107`–`:113`].

> "Approximation (1•2), one over the expected Fisher information, would presumably never be applied in practice ... the conditional variance $(1\cdot4)$ is a corresponding property of the observed likelihood: $(1\cdot4)$ is equal to the reciprocal of the observed Fisher information $I(x)$."

**Observed information = negative Hessian** [`:224`].

> "the observed Fisher information $I(x)=-\ddot{l}_{\theta}$"

### Pawitan (2001) — In All Likelihood (Ch. 13, robustness) {#sec-src-pawitan}

[@pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013]. Invoked by the two-RIV callout in @sec-riv and the §sec-derivation
notation note: the score-variance $\mathcal{J}$ vs expected-Hessian $\mathcal{I}$ distinction,
information equality under correct specification, and the TIC penalty
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$.

**$\mathcal{J}$ (score variance), $\mathcal{I}$ (expected Hessian); equal under the true model** [`:14618`, `:14624`, `:14627`].

> $\mathcal{J} \equiv E\left(\partial\log f_{\theta}/\partial\theta\right)\left(\partial\log f_{\theta}/\partial\theta'\right)\big|_{\theta_0}$ [`:14618`]; &nbsp; $\mathcal{I} \equiv -E\,\partial^{2}\log f_{\theta}/\partial\theta\partial\theta'\big|_{\theta_0}$ [`:14624`]; &nbsp; "These two matrices are the same if $f_{\theta_{0}}(x)$ is the true model." [`:14627`]

**Sandwich variance (Theorem 13.2) and information equality** [`:14632`, `:14699`].

> $\sqrt{n}(\widehat{\theta}-\theta_{0}) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \mathcal{I}^{-1}\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$ [`:14632`]; &nbsp; "Assuming the model is correct we get $\mathcal{J}=\mathcal{I}$, and the formula reduces to the usual inverse Fisher information $\mathcal{I}^{-1}$ ... the 'naive' variance formula." [`:14699`]

**Trace corollary $\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$ (the TIC penalty)** [`:14707`]; the §13.6 AIC derivation restates $\mathcal{J}_{k}$ at [`:14977`].

> $E\left\{n(\widehat{\theta}-\theta_{0})'\mathcal{I}(\widehat{\theta}-\theta_{0})\right\} \to \operatorname{trace}\left(\mathcal{I}\mathcal{I}^{-1}\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1}\right) = \operatorname{trace}\left(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1}\right)$

### Konishi & Kitagawa (1996) — Generalised Information Criteria {#sec-src-konishi}

[@konishiGeneralisedInformationCriteria1996]. Invoked by the two-RIV callout in @sec-riv: the TIC/GIC
generalization of AIC's penalty *under misspecification* — the misspecification face of the
$\mathcal{J}$-vs-$\mathcal{I}$ distinction, contrasted with our correctly-specified,
finite-sample case.

**Theorem 2.1 — bias under possible misspecification** [`:80`–`:90`].

> "Suppose that the parametric family of distributions does not necessarily contain the true distribution ... the asymptotic bias of the log likelihood ... $E_{G}\left\{\eta(X_{n};\hat{G})-\eta(X_{n};G)\right\}=\frac{1}{n} b_{1}(G)+o(1/n)$" [`:80`–`:84`], with $b_{1}(G)=\operatorname{tr}\left\{\int T^{(1)}(z;G)\,\partial\log f(z\mid\theta)/\partial\theta'\big|_{T(G)}\,dG(z)\right\}$ [`:90`].

**GIC = bias-corrected log-likelihood (a trace penalty)** [`:100`–`:101`].

> $\operatorname{GIC}(X_{n};\hat{G}) := -2\sum_{\alpha}\log f(X_{\alpha}\mid\hat{\theta}) + \frac{2}{n}\sum_{\alpha}\operatorname{tr}\left\{T^{(1)}(X_{\alpha};\hat{G})\,\partial\log f(X_{\alpha}\mid\theta)/\partial\theta'\big|_{\hat{\theta}}\right\}$

---

## Appendix B — Numerical and symbolic verification {#sec-verify}

The margin `[mc]` flags throughout the preceding sections link to the executable listings
in this appendix. The code runs inline at render time (SymPy + NumPy); every identity is
checked by reducing `lhs - rhs` to `0` (symbolic) or asserting a match to the R code
`verification/00-setup.R` (numeric, to machine precision). This is the algebraic companion
to the Monte Carlo study (`verification/W1-sweep.R`), which confirms
$\mathbb{E}[T]\approx+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ at many concrete
configurations; here we check the chain of identities that produces it.

Scope: the trace bookkeeping that combines the asymptotic second-moment inputs into
$+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is **symbolic** (exact, parameterization-free);
the MVN closed forms for $I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}},\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
and the FIML Q-function are reimplemented in NumPy and checked **to machine precision**
against the R code on a fixed replicate; and $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is
checked **within Monte-Carlo SE** of the W1 cache $\mathbb{E}[T]$. What Sympy does not
prove are the second-moment inputs themselves (the asymptotic covariances L1-L4); those
are likelihood asymptotics stated as lemmas in @sec-ledger and validated end-to-end by the
Monte Carlo study.

```{python}
import sympy as sp
import numpy as np
from sympy import MatMul, MatAdd, Identity, Trace, Transpose, S
from sympy.matrices.expressions.matexpr import MatrixExpr, MatrixSymbol
from numpy.linalg import inv, slogdet, eigvalsh
from pathlib import Path
import json

print("sympy", sp.__version__, "| numpy", np.__version__)
```

### vech / duplication-matrix machinery {#sec-verify-vech}

MVN running example: $X \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu, \Sigma)$, parameter
$\theta = (\mu,\ \operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ of dimension $k = p + p(p+1)/2$ (D1, D14).
Sympy has no vech/duplication operator, so we build $D_p$ explicitly, mirroring
`00-setup.R` (column-major lower triangle).

```{python}
def vech_index_table(p):
    m = np.zeros((p, p), int); c = 0
    for j in range(p):
        for i in range(j, p):
            c += 1; m[i, j] = c; m[j, i] = c
    return m

def duplication_matrix(p, sym=False):
    kk = p * (p + 1) // 2
    D = sp.zeros(p * p, kk) if sym else np.zeros((p * p, kk))
    c = 0
    for j in range(p):
        for i in range(j, p):
            D[j * p + i, c] = 1
            if i != j:
                D[i * p + j, c] = 1
            c += 1
    return D
```

### The trace normal form (load-bearing helper) {#sec-verify-tracenorm}

Sympy's `Trace` does not auto-apply linearity, does not evaluate
`Trace(Identity) = n`, and does not exploit cyclic invariance `Trace(AB) = Trace(BA)`;
worse, `sympy.simplify` on a `Trace` hits a `signsimp`/`cancel` bug and raises. The
helper below (i) expands matrix products inside each trace, (ii) splits `Trace` over
sums, (iii) evaluates `Trace(c*I) = c*n`, and (iv) canonicalizes cyclic factor order so
cyclically-equal traces collapse. Every symbolic identity is checked after `trace_normal`.

```{python}
def symmetric_reduce(expr):
    """Treat every bare MatrixSymbol as symmetric: Transpose(X) -> X."""
    return expr.replace(
        lambda x: isinstance(x, Transpose) and isinstance(x.arg, MatrixSymbol),
        lambda x: x.arg)

def trace_normal(expr, n):
    """Normal form for scalar Trace(MatMul) expressions (avoids sympy.simplify)."""
    expr = sp.expand(symmetric_reduce(sp.expand(expr, trace=True)), trace=True)

    def handle(t):
        m = symmetric_reduce(sp.expand(t.arg))
        if isinstance(m, MatAdd):
            return sum(handle(Trace(a)) for a in m.args)
        coeff = S.One
        if isinstance(m, MatMul):
            for a in m.args:
                if not isinstance(a, MatrixExpr):
                    coeff *= a
            mats = [a for a in m.args
                    if isinstance(a, MatrixExpr) and not isinstance(a, Identity)]
            if not mats:
                return coeff * n
            rotations = [mats[i:] + mats[:i] for i in range(len(mats))]
            best = min(rotations, key=lambda fs: tuple(sp.srepr(x) for x in fs))
            return coeff * Trace(MatMul(*best))
        if isinstance(m, Identity):
            return coeff * n
        return coeff * Trace(m)

    return expr.replace(lambda x: isinstance(x, Trace), handle)
```

### Term-combination identities (abstract, exact) {#sec-verify-terms}

Treat $I_{\text{com}}, I_{\text{obs}}$ as abstract symmetric positive-definite
$n\times n$ matrices ($n=k$). The asymptotic second-moment inputs (Lemmas L1-L4 of
@sec-ledger, proved there, not here) feed the trace bookkeeping. First, `tr(RIV)`
(@eq-trriv) in normal form:

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-trriv
#| lst-cap: "tr(RIV) = tr(I_obs^-1 I_com) - n, in trace normal form (D8)."
n = sp.Symbol('n', positive=True, integer=True)
I_com = sp.MatrixSymbol('I_com', n, n)
I_obs = sp.MatrixSymbol('I_obs', n, n)
I_mis = I_com - I_obs                      # I_{mis|obs} := I_com - I_obs

trRIV = trace_normal(Trace(I_obs.inv() * I_mis), n)
print("tr(RIV)  =", trRIV)                  # = Trace(I_obs^{-1} I_com) - n
```

**Term A** $= +\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — plug L1 into L3, then cyclic invariance
(@eq-termA):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-terma
#| lst-cap: "Term A: A - tr(RIV) reduces to 0 (symbolic)."
A = trace_normal(Trace(I_mis * I_obs.inv()), n)         # (L3) with (L1)
assert sp.expand(A - trRIV) == 0
print("A        =", A, "  -> A - tr(RIV) =", sp.expand(A - trRIV))
```

**Term B** $= -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — plug L2 into L4; the $n$ ($=k$)
terms cancel, leaving **no** dimension constant (@eq-termB; this is the cancellation the
Term B / L2 disambiguation in the body warns about):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-termb
#| lst-cap: "Term B: B + tr(RIV)/2 reduces to 0, no leftover -k/2 (symbolic)."
B = trace_normal(sp.Rational(-1, 2) * Trace(I_com * (I_obs.inv() - I_com.inv())), n)
assert sp.expand(B + trRIV / 2) == 0
print("B        =", B, "  -> B + tr(RIV)/2 =", sp.expand(B + trRIV / 2))
```

**Combine** $T = A + B = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (@eq-combine, @eq-theorem):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-combine
#| lst-cap: "Combine: T = A + B = tr(RIV)/2, exact (symbolic)."
T = trace_normal(A, n) + B
assert sp.expand(T - trRIV / 2) == 0
print("T = A+B  =", sp.expand(T))
print("T - tr(RIV)/2 =", sp.expand(T - trRIV / 2), " <- exact zero")
```

### MVN Fisher-information closed forms (symbolic, $p=2$) {#sec-verify-mvn}

Block structure of $I_{\text{com}}$ (D15): block-diagonal on $(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$
with $I_\mu = N\Sigma^{-1}$ and $I_{\operatorname{vech}\Sigma} = \tfrac{N}{2}D_p^\top(\Sigma^{-1}\otimes\Sigma^{-1})D_p$.

```{python}
s11, s12, s22, Nsym = sp.symbols('sigma11 sigma12 sigma22 N', positive=True)
Sig2 = sp.Matrix([[s11, s12], [s12, s22]])
Sig2_inv = Sig2.inv()
D2 = duplication_matrix(2, sym=True)
I_mu_2 = Nsym * Sig2_inv
I_sig_2 = sp.Rational(1, 2) * Nsym * D2.T * sp.Matrix(
    sp.kronecker_product(Sig2_inv, Sig2_inv)) * D2
I_sig_2 = sp.simplify(I_sig_2)   # safe here: ordinary Matrix, not Trace
I_mu_2, I_sig_2
```

### NumPy reimplementations checked against R {#sec-verify-numpy}

These mirror `fisher_info_com_mvn`, `fisher_info_obs_mvn`, `loglik_mvn`, and
`barQ_fiml_analytic` in `verification/00-setup.R`. Matching the R outputs on a fixed
replicate is the gate that the symbolic construction equals the code the W1 simulations
actually ran.

```{python}
def fisher_com(mu, Sig, N):
    p = len(mu); Si = inv(Sig); D = duplication_matrix(p)
    kk = p + p * (p + 1) // 2; I = np.zeros((kk, kk))
    I[:p, :p] = N * Si
    I[p:, p:] = (N / 2) * D.T @ np.kron(Si, Si) @ D
    return I

def fisher_obs(Sig, R):
    p = Sig.shape[0]; kk = p + p * (p + 1) // 2
    vi = vech_index_table(p); I = np.zeros((kk, kk))
    pats = {}
    for r in range(R.shape[0]):
        key = tuple(R[r].astype(int)); pats[key] = pats.get(key, 0) + 1
    for key, npat in pats.items():
        Oi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 0]; q = len(Oi)
        if q == 0:
            continue
        Sooi = inv(Sig[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)])
        for a in range(q):
            for b in range(q):
                I[Oi[a], Oi[b]] += npat * Sooi[a, b]
        Dq = duplication_matrix(q)
        Isub = 0.5 * Dq.T @ np.kron(Sooi, Sooi) @ Dq
        full = np.array([p + vi[Oi[a], Oi[b]] - 1
                         for b in range(q) for a in range(b, q)])
        I[np.ix_(full, full)] += npat * Isub
    return I

def loglik(mu, Sig, X):
    Si = inv(Sig); _, ld = slogdet(Sig); hl = 0.5 * np.log(2 * np.pi); p = len(mu)
    d = X - mu
    return float(np.sum(-p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * np.einsum('ij,jk,ik->i', d, Si, d)))

def barQ_fiml(mu_e, Sig_e, mu_i, Sig_i, Y, R):
    Np, p = Y.shape
    Sei = inv(Sig_e); _, ld = slogdet(Sig_e); hl = 0.5 * np.log(2 * np.pi)
    total = 0.0; pats = {}
    for r in range(Np):
        key = tuple(R[r].astype(int)); pats.setdefault(key, []).append(r)
    for key, rows in pats.items():
        Mi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 1]
        Oi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 0]
        if not Mi:                              # fully observed
            for r in rows:
                d = Y[r] - mu_e
                total += -p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * d @ Sei @ d
        elif not Oi:                            # all missing
            d = mu_i - mu_e; tr = np.trace(Sei @ Sig_i)
            total += len(rows) * (-p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * d @ Sei @ d - 0.5 * tr)
        else:                                   # partial: Schur conditional
            Sooi = inv(Sig_i[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)]); Smo = Sig_i[np.ix_(Mi, Oi)]
            condV = Sig_i[np.ix_(Mi, Mi)] - Smo @ Sooi @ Smo.T
            tr = np.trace(Sei[np.ix_(Mi, Mi)] @ condV)
            for r in rows:
                cm = mu_i[Mi] + Smo @ Sooi @ (Y[r, Oi] - mu_i[Oi])
                Xh = np.zeros(p)
                for o in Oi:
                    Xh[o] = Y[r, o]
                for jj, mm in enumerate(Mi):
                    Xh[mm] = cm[jj]
                d = Xh - mu_e
                total += -p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * d @ Sei @ d - 0.5 * tr
    return total
```

The fixture `proof-verification-fixture.json` is one W1 replicate (N=500, non-monotone
MAR) plus the `W1-sweep-prod` cell aggregates, emitted by `verification/W1-sympy-fixture.R`.

```{python}
cands = [Path("proof-verification-fixture.json"),
         Path("manuscript/proof-verification-fixture.json")]
fix = next(p for p in cands if p.exists())
J = json.load(open(fix))

def as_float(x):
    try:
        return float(x)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        return np.nan       # jsonlite serializes missing entries of Y as "NA"

p, k, N = J["meta"]["p"], J["meta"]["k"], J["meta"]["N"]
X = np.array(J["data"]["X"], float)
Y = np.array([[as_float(v) for v in row] for row in J["data"]["Y"]])
R = np.array(J["data"]["R"], float)
mu_obs = np.array(J["theta_obs"]["mu"], float)
Sig_obs = np.array(J["theta_obs"]["Sigma"], float)
mu_com = np.array(J["theta_com"]["mu"], float)
Sig_com = np.array(J["theta_com"]["Sigma"], float)
```
